input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is the MABI MAX 100 serial robot (6-axis) from MABI Robotic AG, specifically the first vertical axis at the base driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor and cycloidal gear. Operational domain is industrial robotics, with key characteristics including precise mechanical components where friction modeling is critical for control accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Services include real-time monitoring of mechanical parameters (e.g., friction) via data-driven identification and optimization of robot control accuracy through derived friction models. Target users are robotics engineers and control system developers, with capabilities to process sensor data for refining control algorithms and enhancing system performance.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including SysML for system modeling across multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are employed for component-and-connector perspectives, describing system components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is enhanced using MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, integrated with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed behavior specification.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time processing of sensor data streams as time series for live measurements, with synchronization between physical and digital components requiring low-latency handling of continuous data flow; specific frequency or latency values are not quantified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Shadow system's architecture comprises an Asset component representing the physical entity, a Model for digital representation, a Processing component that aggregates and abstracts data traces, and DataTrace for contextual data storage. These components integrate through one-way data flow where Processing consumes input data traces and produces output data for the Model.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems using Asset Administration Shells (AAS) as an interoperability standard, MQTT protocol for sensor data streaming, and connection to Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for industrial control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for structural modeling and behavioral specification via state machines. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define component interfaces, interconnections, and composition across abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical). Behavioral detail is enhanced using MontiArc Automaton, MathWorks Simulink simulations, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java), while methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT manage system complexity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: MQTT protocol is used for automated one-way data transmission from physical components to the digital shadow, facilitating real-time state synchronization of sensor data and system states as specified in the Digital Shadow Reference Model.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT is the communication protocol for network connectivity between physical and digital components. Details on network topology, bandwidth, latency requirements, reliability mechanisms, and security protocols are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process involves Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, Use or creation of calculations or models, and continuous improvement of both white- and black-box friction models to enhance accuracy and adaptability.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system generates insights such as joint friction identification and physical relationship understanding through analytics processing components, with user interaction via dashboard visualizations for real-time monitoring and decision support.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system utilizes industry-standard modeling languages and architecture description languages (ADLs) such as SysML [17], ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25,26], and SysML IBDs [17,27], ensuring component-based system representation, interoperability, and integration of heterogeneous stakeholders' perspectives.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the specific robot model, manufacturer, axis type, drive train components (motor and gear), and the operational focus on friction for control accuracy, all of which are explicitly detailed in the source text. Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes from the Swiss company MABI Robotic AG', 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'followed by a cycloidal gear', 'modeling the friction in the robot joints... can cause up to 30% of inaccuracies'.

The description correctly captures the core service of identifying joint friction to improve motion accuracy and the use of live/sensor data. While the specific target users ('robotics engineers and control system developers') are not explicitly named in the text (only referred to generally as 'stakeholders'), the technical description of the services is well-supported. Quotes: 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', 'enhance the robot’s motion accuracy', 'digital shadow consists of several data series... link the existing measurement data', 'improve the robot control’s accuracy'.

The extraction is comprehensive and directly supported by the text. Section II.A describes MBSE usage: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML...'. It lists the abstraction levels: 'requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer'. It details the ADLs: 'component & connector perspective, e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc or SysML IBDs'. It lists behavior tools: 'MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML v1.7... MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'. Section IV.A confirms the use of 'executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the real-time nature of the system, the use of time series, and the streaming of sensor data via MQTT for live measurements. It correctly notes that specific latency values are not quantified in the document. The concept of synchronization is supported by the goal of linking live data with models, though specific synchronization mechanisms are not detailed.

The extraction correctly identifies that this information is not in the document. The paper focuses on a single digital shadow for a specific asset (the first axis of the MABI robot) and a single instance of the system. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between different twins.

The extraction provides a precise summary of the Digital Shadow Reference Model presented in the text, detailing the Asset, Model, Processing component, and DataTrace. It correctly identifies the one-way data flow and the role of the Processing component in consuming input traces and producing output data.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific external systems and standards used in the architecture, including Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for interoperability, MQTT for sensor streaming, and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for industrial control.

block_3_judge.reasoning:
The extraction accurately details the modeling approach described in Section II.A, including the specific use of MBSE, SysML, various ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc), and simulation tools (Simulink, C++, Java). It matches the source text closely and provides high technical specificity.

Quotes: "Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML", "ACME, AADL, MontiArc... describe the system’s components", "combined with further (modeling) tools such as MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java"

The source documents explicitly describe the physical actuators and control mechanisms, including the motor, gearbox, and the Siemens controller. Stating 'Not in Document' is incorrect. Quotes: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The source documents detail the sensing infrastructure, specifically the position encoders and the availability of sensor data streams. Stating 'Not in Document' is incorrect. Quotes: 'There are two position encoders per drive', 'We have access to time series of all sensors'.

The source documents mention uncertainty sources (backlash, positional uncertainty) and a validation approach based on the deviation between simulated and measured data. Stating 'Not in Document' ignores this relevant information. Quotes: 'reduce backlash and positional uncertainty', 'deviation of simulated data versus measured data'.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The document focuses on the creation and operational use of a digital shadow for an industrial robot. While it briefly mentions the 'development phase' in the context of systems engineering, it does not describe the specific lifecycle phases of the Digital Twin itself, transition management between these phases, or lifecycle governance approaches. The characteristic is correctly identified as 'Not in Document'.

The extraction accurately identifies the engineering process steps described in Section II.C and the goal of continuous improvement from the Abstract. However, it omits the fourth step 'Data identification' mentioned in the text. Quotes: '1) Problem identification', '2) Asset and model analysis', '3) Use or creation of calculations or models', 'continuously improving both white- and black-box friction models'.

The extraction accurately identifies 'joint friction identification' and 'physical relationship understanding' as the key insights. It correctly links these to 'analytics processing components' (the Processing component) and 'dashboard visualizations' for user interaction, all of which are explicitly supported by the text. Quotes: 'identification of joint friction', 'Processing component performs these computational steps', 'visualization in a dashboard'.

The extracted characteristic accurately reflects Section II.A of the source document, which lists industry-standard modeling languages and ADLs such as 'SysML [17]', 'ACME [23]', 'AADL [24]', 'MontiArc [25,26]', and 'SysML IBDs [17,27]'. It correctly identifies their use for 'component-based system representation' and integrating 'heterogeneous stakeholders'.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The document focuses on the technical implementation of a digital shadow for robot friction modeling. It does not contain information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy regulations, or consent management. The extraction 'Not in Document' is correct.

The document describes the robot's physical setup, noting only that 'The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range to [−90°, 90°]'. However, it does not provide details on cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication, or fail-safe mechanisms as defined by the characteristic. Therefore, the extraction 'Not in Document' accurately reflects the lack of substantive information on these topics.
